what_it_does: Defines semantic CSS utility classes .surface--<zone> that map existing design tokens for background, border, and shadow onto panel/card zones, and adds a shared .is-active gold-accent treatment.
observations: Uses an attribute substring selector [class*="surface--"] to apply shared border-radius and relative positioning to all surface classes.
Declares ten semantic zones: base, content, panel, review, source, package, success, warning, rejected, and vi.
Each zone pairs a background token var(--surface-<zone>) with a matching edge token var(--surface-<zone>-edge).
The .is-active modifier on any surface adds var(--shadow-pop), a 1px gold glow outline, and a gold border color.
Comments state that color values live only in colors_and_type.css and are referenced through MS_SURFACES from JS, while this file only wires tokens to classes.
A load-order comment notes the file is loaded after app.css and before theme.css, which recolours the gold system including --surface-vi.
standouts: Semantic surface zoning expressed as modifier utility classes rather than ad-hoc background colors.
Active state is applied generically to every surface zone via a single attribute-selector rule.
Strong claim of single-source token ownership: this file does not introduce new colors.
conventions: Groups shared base styles under an attribute selector rather than a base .surface class.
Documents file load order and token ownership in comments.
concerns: All color, shadow, and accent tokens are defined externally, so missing tokens would leave surfaces transparent/unstyled.
The [class*="surface--"] selector can match any class containing that substring, not just intended surface utilities.
The active-state outline is implemented as an extra box-shadow spread, which can complicate stacking and anti-aliasing.
notes: This file maps design-token surface colors and edges onto semantic panel/card utility classes and adds a unified gold-accent active state.
questions: What is the exact structure of the MS_SURFACES JS object and how it relates to these CSS classes?
Which components consume each semantic zone, and how is .is-active toggled?
Why does only .surface--content receive an explicit card shadow?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches its stated purpose: it wires existing single-sourced surface tokens into semantic utility classes and adds an active-state enhancement.
apparent_themes: Semantic zoning over literal color values.
Single source of truth for design tokens.
Subtle elevation and consistent selection feedback.
intention_signals: It appears to be building a token-driven visual language for panels and cards where each workflow-related zone gets a tinted wash and hairline edge, while selected items are brought forward with a uniform gold accent glow.
novelty: Semantic surface zones named after workflow concepts (source, package, review, vi) rather than generic colors; a generic active-state rule applied across all zones via an attribute selector.
contribution: Provides the CSS mapping between design-token surface colors/edges and semantic utility classes, plus a shared active-state treatment, so a fused system can retain the same surface-zone vocabulary and selection glow behavior.
summary_for_embedding: surfaces.css defines semantic utility classes .surface--<zone> that apply token-based translucent backgrounds, hairline borders, and optional shadows, plus a gold-accent active state.
intention_for_embedding: It was meant to give panels and cards a consistent, token-driven surface zoning system with a unified selected-state glow.
